coredata.dc:title: Decorated Ca-Fe-LDHs onto derived olive leaves carbon quantum dots for effective remediation of arsenic (V) from water

coredata.dc:description: Arsenic species in contaminated water are known to cause significant and sever health consequences, featuring skin lesions, cardiovascular illnesses, developmental issues, and various forms of cancer. Therefore, efficient adsorptive removal approaches of arsenic ions from water represent a task with major concern. Hence, the current study is devoted to assemble and synthesize a novel and sustainable 2D nanosorbent with numerous functional groups, via a bottom-up pyrolysis and coprecipitation approach via incorporating olive leaf-derived carbon quantum dots (OCQDs) with calcium-iron layered double hydroxides (CaFe-LDH) matrix for the generation of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs. The resulting nanosorbent was characterized with particle size between 5.246–7.869 nm and confirmed by EDX analysis to contain Ca (2.48 %), Fe (1.04 %), C (54.81 %), and O (41.66 %). The FT-IR spectroscopic analysis validated the successful formation of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs, revealing several functional groups (C–H, –OH, N–H, CO and metal–oxygen bonds) along with carbonate and goethite-related vibrations that suggest a layered structure with reactive surface characteristics. Moreover, the XPS analysis revealed the prevalence of Ca, N, Fe, O, and C, indicating successful formation. The XRD monitoring of OCQDs@CaFe-LDHs referred to the presence of various structural phases, including layered double hydroxide, calcite, and iron oxides in a well-crystallized structure to enhance its adsorption capabilities. Consequently, As(V) removal by the investigated OCQDs@CaFe-LDHs was testified and optimized under diverse controlling conditions and the collected findings from this investigation referred to the optimum performance at pH 1.0, 30 min contact time, 150 mg dosage, and 25 °C reaction temperature. Ionic strength parameter demonstrated remarkable selectivity of the prepared nanosorbent towards As(V) ions. Moreover, As(V) removing at pH 2 condition, achieved 77.8 % and 71.5 % by using 5 and 10 mg/L at 60 min, respectively. The adsorption binding mechanisms were correlated to both ion-pair and anion-exchange interactions. This was mainly configured on the basis of isotherm and kinetic studies which revealed that the reaction of As(V) ions with OCQDs@CaFe-LDHs was mostly validated by the Elovich and pseudo-second-order models, while Freundlich and Flory-Huggins isotherms were verified as excellent models denoting to the nanosorbent surface heterogeneity. Thermodynamic parameters highlighted the exothermic and spontaneous nature of the reaction. OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs exhibited excellent stability across three regeneration cycles, maintaining 90.3 % removal of As(V) after the 3rd cycle, and thus referring to the high suitability for repeated arsenic remediation. OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs achieved As(V) removal up to 98.6 % in both tap water and industrial wastewater, demonstrating high potential for decontamination of As(V) from polluted water sources by providing 97.8 % (5 mg/L) and 98.4 % (10 mg/L) from tap water, as well as 98.1 % (5 mg/L) and 98.6 % (10 mg/L) from wastewater. Finally, the efficient performance of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs could be listed as an unprecedented and promising nanosorbent in successful removal of arsenic ions from various polluted wastewaters.

head.ce:title: Decorated Ca-Fe-LDHs onto derived olive leaves carbon quantum dots for effective remediation of arsenic (V) from water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Arsenic species in contaminated water are known to cause significant and sever health consequences, featuring skin lesions, cardiovascular illnesses, developmental issues, and various forms of cancer. Therefore, efficient adsorptive removal approaches of arsenic ions from water represent a task with major concern. Hence, the current study is devoted to assemble and synthesize a novel and sustainable 2D nanosorbent with numerous functional groups, via a bottom-up pyrolysis and coprecipitation approach via incorporating olive leaf-derived carbon quantum dots (OCQDs) with calcium-iron layered double hydroxides (CaFe-LDH) matrix for the generation of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs. The resulting nanosorbent was characterized with particle size between 5.246–7.869 nm and confirmed by EDX analysis to contain Ca (2.48 %), Fe (1.04 %), C (54.81 %), and O (41.66 %). The FT-IR spectroscopic analysis validated the successful formation of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs, revealing several functional groups (C–H, –OH, N–H, CO and metal–oxygen bonds) along with carbonate and goethite-related vibrations that suggest a layered structure with reactive surface characteristics. Moreover, the XPS analysis revealed the prevalence of Ca, N, Fe, O, and C, indicating successful formation. The XRD monitoring of OCQDs@CaFe-LDHs referred to the presence of various structural phases, including layered double hydroxide, calcite, and iron oxides in a well-crystallized structure to enhance its adsorption capabilities. Consequently, As(V) removal by the investigated OCQDs@CaFe-LDHs was testified and optimized under diverse controlling conditions and the collected findings from this investigation referred to the optimum performance at pH 1.0, 30 min contact time, 150 mg dosage, and 25 °C reaction temperature. Ionic strength parameter demonstrated remarkable selectivity of the prepared nanosorbent towards As(V) ions. Moreover, As(V) removing at pH 2 condition, achieved 77.8 % and 71.5 % by using 5 and 10 mg/L at 60 min, respectively. The adsorption binding mechanisms were correlated to both ion-pair and anion-exchange interactions. This was mainly configured on the basis of isotherm and kinetic studies which revealed that the reaction of As(V) ions with OCQDs@CaFe-LDHs was mostly validated by the Elovich and pseudo-second-order models, while Freundlich and Flory-Huggins isotherms were verified as excellent models denoting to the nanosorbent surface heterogeneity. Thermodynamic parameters highlighted the exothermic and spontaneous nature of the reaction. OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs exhibited excellent stability across three regeneration cycles, maintaining 90.3 % removal of As(V) after the 3rd cycle, and thus referring to the high suitability for repeated arsenic remediation. OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs achieved As(V) removal up to 98.6 % in both tap water and industrial wastewater, demonstrating high potential for decontamination of As(V) from polluted water sources by providing 97.8 % (5 mg/L) and 98.4 % (10 mg/L) from tap water, as well as 98.1 % (5 mg/L) and 98.6 % (10 mg/L) from wastewater. Finally, the efficient performance of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs could be listed as an unprecedented and promising nanosorbent in successful removal of arsenic ions from various polluted wastewaters.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution is a critical worldwide challenge issue that poses considerable risks to public health, ecosystems, and biodiversity [1]. Among various pollutants, heavy metals are particularly hazardous due to their toxic effects, persistence in the environment, and potential for bioaccumulation [2,3]. These pollutants often originate from multiple sources, including industrial discharges, agricultural runoff containing pesticides and fertilizers, and accidental chemical spills [4]. Arsenic, a naturally occurring chemical element, is a notable heavy metal pollutant with existing two oxidation states, viz as As(III) and As(V) [5]. It is particularly of high concern due to its widespread distribution in groundwater, especially in regions where groundwater serves as the principal source for drinking water, such as Southeast Asia and parts of the United States [6]. Chronic arsenic exposure in contaminated water can cause significant health consequences, featuring skin lesions, cardiovascular illnesses, developmental issues, and various forms of cancer [7]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has recommending a maximum allowable concentration at 10 ppb in drinking water [8]. To effectively remove arsenic from water, numerous techniques have been developed including oxidation–reduction processes, ion-exchange and chemical precipitation. Chemical precipitation involves adding chemicals, but this process requires careful pH control and efficient management of hazardous sludge. Oxidation-reduction processes convert arsenic into less toxic or removable forms, yet they often necessitate chemical reagents and precise control of reaction conditions. Ion-exchange techniques swap harmful metal ions for harmless ones, although they can be costly and require frequent regeneration of the resin [9,10]. Adsorption, on the other hand, has gained prominence as a viable way for removing metal ions from contaminated water [11]. A wide variety of adsorbents are being explored for removal of arsenic, involving traditional materials like activated carbon, clay minerals, as well as advanced nanomaterials [12]. Among these, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are distinctive category of carbonaceous nanoparticles measuring < 10 nm in diameter with high surface area, strong optical characteristics, adequate water solubility, and chemical stability [13,14]. Green natural raw materials could be employed to prepare CQDs including fresh tomatoes, broccoli [15], garlic, dried leaf [16], and Ginkgo leaf [17]. Moreover, numerous experiments have been conducted utilizing leaves or peels as raw materials to synthesize different fluorescent CQDs [18]. Therefore, the multifunctional nature of CQDs as their potential for surface modifications enhanced their multidiscipline applicability as nanosorbents [19]. Moreover, olive leaves are indeed fascinating, offering both traditional health benefits and modern applications. Their role in nanotechnology, particularly in the synthesis of (CQDs), adds a cutting-edge aspect to their value [20]. The unique characteristics of CQDs offer the potential for surface modifications, with the goal of improving their adsorption capabilities in water treatment purposes [21]. It is evident from the comparison between traditional carbon quantum dots and modified one that there is a significant difference in their adsorption efficiency for removing pollutants from water [22].

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) represent another promising category of adsorbents. These two-dimensional intercalated composites consist of positive charged metal hydroxide layers with anions intercalated to guarantee charge neutrality [23]. LDHs also feature a high interlayer negative charge density, enabling the controlled incorporation of different anions within their structures [24]. Additionally, the adjustable grain size, combined with low production costs and eco-friendliness, makes LDHs a practical choice for environmental remediation [25,26]. As reported, diatomite combined with the altered nickel and ferric LDHs efficiently adsorbed both As(III) and As(V), while zinc ferric LDH was utilized for the removal of arsenite [27,28]. Additionally, functionalized magnesium and aluminum LDHs integrated with magnetic hollow Fe3O4 have shown effectiveness in arsenate removal [29,30]. Furthermore, bimetallic LDHs demonstrated effectiveness as adsorbents to the detoxification of As(V) from water matrices [31]. However, LDHs have very critical drawbacks, including their low stability in acidic environments and the potential for releasing adsorbed pollutants back into water over time [28] due to their ultra-thin thickness structural. Also, the presence of limited active sites is often attributed to their tendencies for agglomeration [32]. Thus, it is essential to modify LDHs to enhance their chemical performance.

Combining CQDs with LDHs is expected to synergistically enhance their properties for environmental remediation. This hybridization can improve the adsorption capability and catalytic activity for various contaminants [33]. Hence, the current study aims to develop an innovative, eco-friendly nanosorbent (OCQDs@Ca-Fe LDHs) via integrating olive leaf-derived OCQDs with Ca-Fe layered double hydroxides. Such generated hybrid structure combines the properties of metal oxyhydroxides, metal oxides, and carbonaceous anions, resulting in a mesoporous, crystalline composite with high stability and efficiency in As(V) adsorption. In addition, a comprehensive characterization of the assembled OCQDs@Ca-Fe LDHs nanosorbent is also aimed via FT-IR, SEM-EDX, XRD, and XPS to verify the structural features. The adsorption performance is systematically examined and optimized under diverse conditions of pH, As(V) concentration, nanosorbent dosage, reaction duration, temperature, and ionic strength. Kinetic, isotherm, as well thermodynamic analyses are further illuminated the adsorption mechanisms of OCQDs@Ca-Fe LDHs, supporting its suitability as a sustainable, cost-effective nanosorbent for water treatment.
Table 1S (Supplementary material) compiles a list of all applicable instruments for analysis and characterization.

Olive carbon quantum dots (OCQDs) was synthesized based on some collected olive leaves as the carbon source. Calcium chloride (CaCl2) was obtained from Merck (Germany), while ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) and (KOH) potassium hydroxide was purchased from Fisher Scientific. Sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) was supplied by Chem-Lab (Belgium), and formaldehyde was used in the preparation of Ca-Fe LDHs. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %) was sourced from Thermo Scientific (Germany), while (NaOH) sodium hydroxide was employed for pH adjustment. Sodium chloride (NaCl) from Sigma-Aldrich (USA) was employed to explore the effectiveness of ionic strength on the dismissal of As(V) by OCQDs@Ca-Fe LDHs nanosorbent. Ultrapure distilled water was used in all solution preparations. Wastewater samples were collected from the Middle East Oil Refinery (MIDOR) in Egypt for testing adsorption performance. A mono-element calibration standard arsenic solution was acquired from CPAchem (Bulgaria), and concentrated nitric acid (HNO3, 69 %) was utilized to prepare standard arsenic solutions (5, 10 and 25 mg/L).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of OCQDs, Ca-Fe-LDHs and OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs
ce:section.ce:para: A muffle furnace was utilized for one-step pyrolysis to synthesize OCQDs from olive leaves, a cost-effective and sustainable precursor. The olive leaves were washed many times and afterwards dried as well as grinded in a mixer. 25 g powder was pyrolyzed for 1 h at 300 °C, transforming it to a black substance, yielding purified OCQDs [34].

To synthesize (Ca-Fe-LDHs), 16.65 g (0.15 mol) of calcium chloride (CaCl2) and 6.675 g (0.05 mol) ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) were mixed and dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water (DW) with gentle heating and stirring. A separate solution containing 50 mL in distilled water was mixed with 8.4 g (0.15 mol) KOH and 5.3 g (0.05 mol) Na2CO3. This solution was added dropwise to the previously prepared CaCl2 and FeCl3 mixture while stirring continuously and maintaining the temperature at 60 °C for an hour. Subsequently, the obtained Ca-Fe LDHs product was left to age at 80 °C for 2 h. After aging, the product was filtered and washed repeatedly with distilled water till a neutral pH was achieved. Eventually, dry the product at 70 °C until it reaches absolute dryness.

As illustrated in (Fig. 1 ), OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs nanosorbent was synthesized by combining 2.0 g of OCQDs with 4.0 g of Ca-Fe LDHs in a 1:2 mass ratio. 10 mL of formaldehyde and 50 mL of (DW) were added to this mixture. This was refluxed for 5 h, after which the product was filtered, washed with (DW), and finally dried at 70 °C until fully dry.

The uptake behavior and performance of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs nanosorbent in As(V) removal were investigated through the batch mode experiments. The removal efficiency (R%) was evaluated by varying several parameters, including pH (1–10), As(V) concentration (5–100 mg/L), reaction time (1–60 min), ionic strength (10–100 mg NaCl), nanosorbent dosage (10–150 mg), and temperature (25–55 °C). The surface charge of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was determined by measurement the zeta potentials in absence of As(V)) and in presence of loaded As(V)) on the nanosorbent surface.

The variation impact of pH was tracked by mixing 10.0 mg of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs in 20 mL solution (5 mg/L As(V)) at the desired pH, adjusted using 0.1 mol/L NaOH or HCl. The solution was shaken at room temperature for 60 min. After filtration and by using a syringe filters with a pore size of 0.22 µm, the As(V) concentration was measured at λmax = 193 nm by PerkinElmer ICP-OES instrument. The (%R) removal efficiency as well as adsorption capacity (qe) in mg/g values were then determined to assess the impact of pH onto the adsorption process according to Eq. (1).

The influence of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs mass on As(V) disposal was studied by adding different masses from 10 to 150 mg of the nanosorbent to 20 mL As(V) solutions with initial concentrations (5, 10, and 25 mg/L) at optimum pH 2.0. After shaking the mixtures for 60 min, the nanosorbent was separated, and the absorbance was measured to compute the (%R) removal efficiency values from Eq. (1).

To explore the impact of ionic strength on As(V) disposal, 150 mg of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was mixed with 20 mL As(V) solutions (5&10 mg/L) at pH 2.0, followed by the addition of NaCl using concentration from 10 to 100 mg. The mixture was shaken for 60 min, and after separating the nanosorbent, the remaining As(V) concentration was measured at λmax = 193 nm to compute the (%R) removal efficiency values from Eq. (1).

The influence of reaction time on As(V) removal was investigated through adding 150 mg OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs to 20 mL of As(V) (5 and 10 mg/L) at pH 2.0. After varying time intervals, the nanosorbent was filtered and the final As(V) concentrations were measured at λmax = 193 nm. To determine the underlying mechanisms of adsorption, several kinetic models were applied and analyzed for the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs system.

The influence of initial As(V) concentration was studied using ICP-OES instrument at λmax = 193 nm in the confines of 5–100 mg/L at pH 2.0, within a reaction time at 60 min, using 150 mg of the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs. Adsorption isotherms were evaluated based on the models of Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin and Flory-Huggins to understand the adsorption capacity, behavior and interaction mechanisms.

The impact of temperature on As(V) disposal was explored at temperatures 25–55 °C. A 20 mL As(V) solution (5&10 mg/L) at pH 2.0, was combined with 150 mg of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs and agitated for 60 min at the specified temperatures. After filtration, the final As(V) concentration was measured at λmax = 193 nm. The adsorption affinity of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs for As(V) was evaluated by the calculation of thermodynamic parameters, viz. (ΔG°) Gibbs free energy, (ΔH°) enthalpy and (ΔS°) entropy.

OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs efficiency in removing As(V) from actual water samples, including tap water and industrial wastewater was monitored. 150 mg of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was added to each 20 mL (5&10 mg/L As(V)) at pH 2.0 and shaken for 60 min and finally filtered. Eq. (1) was used to compute the removal efficiency (R%) in order to assess the composite effectiveness in real-world applications.

Recyclability of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs nanosorbent was accomplished by reacting 150.0 mg with 40 mL of adjusted 10 mg/L As(V) solution at pH 2.0 for 60 min of shaking. The treated nanosorbent was filtered and allowed to dry. The surface loaded As(V) on OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was eluted using 0.1 mol L−1 HCl [35], and the remaining OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs were carefully washed and dried. To evaluate the effectiveness of the recycled OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs in eliminating As(V), this procedure was carried out three cycles to determine its suitability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and surface features of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR bands of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs exhibited several peaks at different wavenumbers (3398, 2927, 2871, 2513, 1795, 1599, 1444, 1110, 1028, 875, 787, 712, 619, and 466 cm−1), that align with various functional groups as precisely displayed in (Fig. 2 a). Prominent peak at 3398 cm−1 is indicative of –OH stretching, reflecting the presence of moisture onto the surface of this nanosorbent. The peaks occur at 2927 and 2871 cm−1 are related to stretching vibrations of C–H aliphatic groups [36]. The 2513 cm−1 peak along with the sharp peak at 1795 cm−1, implies the presence of calcite (CaCO3. The 1599 cm−1 peak fits with the bending modes of N–H and O–H bond, while the peak at 1444 cm−1 corresponds to CH2 bending vibration. The 1110 and 1028 cm−1 peaks respectively reflect C–O and C-N stretching vibrations. The CO bonds found in the carbonate anion are reflected in the two peaks exactly at 875 and 712 cm−1. The peak located at 787 cm−1 is ascribed to metal–oxygen (M−O) bond vibrations or carbonate species [24]. The 466 cm−1 peak is ascribed to Fe–O or Ca–O stretching vibrations. The 619 cm−1 is related to Fe–O vibrations characteristic of goethite within the metal hydroxide lattice, further supporting the layered structure of the composite [37]. Consequently, the final results of FT-IR confirm that OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was successfully prepared.

As shown in Fig. 2b, the XRD analysis of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs reveals key structural features, with peaks indicating various phases within this nanosorbent. The peak at 23° is equivalent to the (003) plane of (LDH), indicating interlayer spacing and presence of water. The 29° peak suggests the (104) plane of calcite (CaCO3) (JCPDS card number 01–072-1937) or Fe2O3 (JCPDS card number 01–079-0007), confirming the existence of these phases within the composite. The peak at 30° may indicate the (110) plane of Ca(OH)2 formation (JCPDS card number 03–05492), where the peak at 37° correlates to the (110) reflection of Fe2O3, reinforcing the presence of iron oxide in the sample, which is crucial for its magnetic and catalytic properties [38]. The peak at 39° can be credited to the (111) reflection of Fe2O3, implying potential phase transitions in the iron species. The peak at 40° supports the existence of Fe2O3, while the peak at 43° relates to the (220) reflection of iron oxides, providing insights into the crystallinity of the iron component. The peak at 47° may correspond to the (310) reflection of Fe2O3, indicating the material magnetic properties. The peak at 50° correspond to Fe3O4 (JCPDS card number 00-019-0629), while 57° and 62° peaks may indicate the (202) and (211) planes of Fe2O3, respectively, confirming additional iron oxide phases and structural complexity [39]. Importantly, the existence of OCQDs in the structure of the composite optimizes its functionality, positively impacting its adsorption capabilities and overall performance in applications. Therefore, the XRD of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs clarified a good crystalline structure, ensuring the successful modification process.

The chemical compositions of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs were verified from (EDX) energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy in Fig. 2c. Given that OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs contains mainly carbon (54.81 %), oxygen (41.66 %), calcium (2.48 %) and iron (1.04 %). The ways that OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs adsorbs metal samples and their florescence characteristics are influenced by the chemical structure.

The particle size and morphology of OCQD@Ca-Fe-LDHs were evaluated from the SEM analysis, as demonstrated in Fig. 2d. The SEM images reveal the characteristic layered homogenous structure of OCQD@Ca-Fe-LDHs [40]. The observed particle sizes ranged from 5 to 8 nm, with noticeable porosity that enhances surface reactivity for effective As(V) adsorption.

The XPS analysis was applied to identify the elemental composition and surface valence state of the synthesized materials. The XPS survey (Fig. 3 a) signified the abundance of Ca, Fe, C, O and N. As obtained for C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b), the deconvoluted peaks reveal three primary carbon environments. The peak at 284.8 eV correlates with C–C/C–H bonds, indicating graphitic or aliphatic carbon, which is typical for carbon-based quantum dots [41]. The 286.7 eV peak delegated to C–O bonds. At 289.2 eV peak related with OC–O bonds, suggesting carboxyl or ester groups. These functional groups enhance the composite surface reactivity and hydrophilicity, promoting effective As(V) adsorption [42]. The O1s summit observed at 529.83 eV reveals the presence of metal oxide (M−O) bonds [43]. The band at 531.79 eV corresponds with metal hydroxide bonds (M−OH) and is associated with CO groups also [44]. The band at 534.0 eV signifies the existence of surface hydroxyl groups (M−OH) and other oxygen incorporating functional groups as –O– or −CO in the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs. This peak reflects the composite complex surface chemistry, which enhances its reactivity and adsorption performance (Fig. 3c) [45]. Peaks at 339.9 and 402.2 eV in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) typically indicate the presence of nitrogen species in the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs composite. The peak at 339.9 eV may correspond to nitrogen in amine or amide groups, while the peak at 402.2 eV is often linked with nitrogen in aromatic or heterocyclic compounds [46]. The Ca2p spectrum (Fig. 3e) illustrates three distinct peaks at 347.1, 350.3 and 353.6 eV, associated with Ca2p3/2 and Ca2p1/2 spin–orbit splitting, indicating the presence of Ca in different chemical environments. The 347.1 and 350.3 eV match with Ca–O bonding within the LDHs structure, where the higher peak binding energy at 353.6 eV may suggest modified Ca sites influenced by OCQDs [47]. Fe2p spectrum (Fig. 3f) reveals peaks at 710.6, 713.6, 717.0, 720.4, 724.3, 727.7, and 732.1 eV, indicating the presence of Fe3+ in iron oxide or hydroxide forms. The main peaks at 710.6 and 713.6 eV are related to Fe3+ (Fe2p3/2) typically seen in Fe2O3 or FeOOH, while the nearby peaks at 717 and 720.4 eV are characteristic satellite peaks, further supporting the Fe3+ assignment. The peaks at 724.3 and 727.7 eV represent the Fe2p1/2 region for Fe3+, with an additional satellite peak at 732.1 eV confirming this oxidation state. These findings suggest that Fe in the composite exists mainly as Fe3+, which contributes to adsorption properties through the iron oxide/hydroxide structure [48]. Finally, the characterized BET results of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs referred to 258.4 m2 g−1 (surface area), 5.3158 × 10−2 cm3 g−1 (total pore volume) and 8.230 nm (mean pore diameter).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorptive removal investigations of As(V) by OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs

ce:section.ce:para: Adsorption process can be influenced by varying pH levels of the solution. Adjusting the pH can regulate the binding active sites availability and alter the adsorbent surface charge. As a consequence, this modification affects the interaction or binding efficiency between the adsorbent and the target adsorbate [49]. Fig. 4 compiles and show the experimental results about how pH affects adsorption performance. As obtained, the removal efficiency of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was evaluated over a pH range from 1 to 10. Maximum adsorption efficiency was attained at pH 1, where the uptake percentages were 23.8 %, 47.2 %, 64.0 %, and 91.7 % for 10, 50, 100, and 150 mg of the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs composite, respectively, in the existence of 5 mg/L As(V). The adsorption efficiency progressively declined as the pH increased, reaching its minimum at pH 10, with uptake percentages of 1.8 %, 3.7 %, 5.2 %, and 7.4 % for the same concentrations, as shown in Fig. 4. In general, As(V) ions predominantly exist as H2AsO4 −1 between pH 2.1 and 6.7, while HAsO4 −2 becomes the dominant species when the pH exceeds 6.7. At low pH 1–2, the surface charge of the arsenic-loaded OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs becomes predominantly positive, facilitating strong electrostatic and ion-pair attractions with As(V) species [50]. Additionally, the presence of inner exchangeable anions, such as CO3 2−and metal hydroxides within the semi-hydrotalcite structure, enhances uptake efficiency through anion-exchange with As(V) ions. However, the decrease of As(V) adsorption at elevated pH levels can be ascribed to the repulsive interactions between the negatively charged surface of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs and the As(V) ions. Furthermore, as the pH rises, the concentration of OH– ions also rises, leading to competition with As(V) ions for available adsorption sites [51]. Finally, the high percentage removal of As(V) at pH 1–2 is closely linked to the surface of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs which was established by the measurement of zeta potential as depicted in Fig. 5 . The OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs exhibited −17.6 mV zeta potential, indicating a relatively stable dispersion due to electrostatic repulsion among particles. However, after loading arsenic at pH 2, the zeta potential of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was increased to + 19.2 mV, indicating a notable shift in surface charge due to excessive presence of hydrogen ions on the surface. This shift suggests that arsenate ions (As(V)) bind to the composite's surface via enhancing the electrostatic and ion-pair interactions, at such low pH condition [52].

One of the most important aspects of adsorption processes for its practical and financial significance is the adsorbent dosage [53]. The impact of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs dosage on As(V) uptake was examined using masses varies between 10 to 150 mg (Fig. 6 ), with the results denoted that the adsorption capabilities were improved by the increase of nanosorbent dosage for As(V) solution concentrations (5, 10 and 25 mg/L) at pH 2.0. This findings can be credited to the increase in the active sites on the external surface of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs, as well as the availability of inner exchangeable anions, which enhances with the addition of more nanosorbent mass [51].

The impact of ionic strength on pollutant removing is crucial for optimizing adsorbent performance in real-world water treatment systems. Competing ions in wastewater can interfere into adsorption by interacting with active sites. Hence, ionic strength significance on the As(V) elimination by OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was investigated using NaCl concentrations ranging from 10.0 to 100.0 mg, as shown in Fig. 7 . The effectiveness of removing As(V) by OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs showed a gradual improvement, increasing from 90.7 % to 96.1 % for 5 mg/L As(V) solution. An analogous trend was noticed for 10 mg/L As(V) concentration, where the removal potency enhanced from 87.6 % to 93.5 % with the use of OCQDs@Ca-Fe LDHs at pH 2.0. This finding may be explained through several mechanisms. First, higher ionic strength reduces electrostatic repulsions among the negatively charged As(V) ions and the composite surface by compressing the electrical double layer and allowing ions to approach adsorption sites more easily. Additionally, Na+ ions may act as bridges, facilitating stronger interactions between As(V) and the adsorbent. The increased ionic strength also promotes faster diffusion of ions into vacant pores to, facilitate and improve the mobility of As(V) ions with lower concentration levels as 5 mg/L towards adsorption into pore sites. Furthermore, the LDH structure of the composite enhanced interlayer anion-exchange, leading to improve As(V) removal. Finally, the (OCQDs) provide additional active sites, and the Na+ ions may stabilize their surface interactions, collectively contributing to the enhanced performance under higher ionic strength conditions [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of reaction time and kinetic studies
ce:section.ce:para: Time of the reaction serves as an essential role in the As(V) removing process by OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs nanosorbent as it exhibit a direct influence on the adsorption kinetics and overall efficacy of removal. This aspect was thoroughly investigated by varying the reactions time from 1 to 120 min at different initial As(V) concentrations (5&10 mg/L), while controlling at related optimal conditions to pH 2.0 and 150 mg mass of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs. It was transpired that the removal of As(V) occurred rapidly at the outset, primarily caused by the high As(V) adsorption onto the active sites of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs and the increased availability of interchangeable carbonate anions within the interlayer structure [55]. Equilibrium was achieved after 30 min, by that time point all active sites and interchangeable anions were saturated by As(V) ions. Following this, the system maintained an equilibrium state for over 30 min, lasting up to 60 min, indicating complete occupation of the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs surface functionalities by As(V) ions. As shown in Fig. 8a , the adsorption efficiencies were raised from 30.3 % and 91.50 % to 75.8 % and 99.3 % at 30 min, respectively for initial As(V) concentrations at 5 and 10 mg/L.

To acquire further insights regarding the most appropriate representation for the adsorption kinetics and to elucidate the governing mechanism of As(V) removal by OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs, various kinetic models were employed, including pseudo-first and second orders, Elovich models, and diffusion of intra-particle. These four kinetic models were evaluated, as outlined in (Table 1 ), with the critical parameters acquired from these models presented in Table 2 S. Regarding the pseudo-first-order model (Fig. 8b and Table 1), data for 5 mg/L As(V) demonstrated a reasonable fit, with a calculated qe of 0.4093 mg/g nearly aligning with the experimental (qe ​= 0.3119 mg/g). The high correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9976 supports this fit. However, at 10 mg/L As(V), the model exhibited significant deviations, yielding a calculated qe ​0.9888 mg/g compared to an experimental qe ​ of only 0.0494 mg/g, alongside a lower R2 of 0.8915. This implies that pseudo first order model is unsuitable at higher concentrations. In contrast, pseudo-second-order model provided a significantly greater fit to both concentrations. As seen in Fig. 8c for 5 mg/L As (V), the calculated qe 0.4093 mg/g is closely aligning with the experimental qe 0.4443 mg/g, with an excellent correlation coefficient R2 = 0.99813. At 10 mg/L As(V), the model showed an almost perfect fit, with a calculated qe 0.9888 mg/g nearly identical to the experimental qe 0.9908 mg/g, and an extremely high R2 = 0.99999. Furthermore, the higher rate constants observed in the pseudo-second-order suggest faster adsorption kinetics, particularly at elevated concentrations. Overall, pseudo-second-order could be listed as more appropriate for explaining the adsorption process at both concentrations, implying that a chemisorption was the dominant mechanism for removal of As(V) by OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs [56].

Based on the correlated parameters from all discussed models, the As(V) adsorption mechanism by OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was mostly driven by chemisorption, as shown by the strong fits of the pseudo- second-order as well Elovich models, indicating chemical interactions via ion-pair and ion-exchange mechanisms. Intraparticle diffusion served a significant role at lower concentrations, but decreased in significance as site saturation occurred at higher concentrations. These findings highlight a complex mechanism involving both chemisorption and diffusion dynamics in the adsorption process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of initial As(V) concentration and adsorption isotherms
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of As(V) initial concentration was examined across a range of 5 to 100 mg/L using ICP-OES instrument at λmax = 193 nm under controlled conditions (dosage = 150 mg, pH = 2, shaking time = 60 min, and 20 mL As(V) solution). As expressed in (Fig. 9 ), the removal effectiveness of As(V) by OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs demonstrated a decreasing trend as the concentration of As(V) increased. Specifically, the removal percentages were recorded at 77.8 %, 71.5 %, 65.4 %, 60.0 %, 54.3 %, 50.3 %, and 37.0 % at initial concentrations respectively for 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 100 mg/L, respectively. This behavior could be defined and related to the saturation of available active sites on the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs nanosorbent [60]. At fewer As(V) concentrations, the active sites and the availability of interchangeable carbonate anions on the surface were sufficient to adsorb most of the As(V) ions, leading to higher removal efficiency. When the As(V) concentration rises, the fixed number of adsorption sites and exchangeable anions become saturated more quickly, resulting in lesser available sites relative to the increasing amount of As(V) ions. Consequently, the adsorption capacity of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs became limited, causing the percentage of As(V) removal to decline [61].

To interpret the As(V) adsorption behavior, four established isotherm models were utilized to characterize the possible interaction with the active sites of the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs (Table 3 S). The first applied model is the Langmuir isotherm which presumes monolayer adsorption on the surface with identical adsorption sites, without interactions between adsorbed species. According to Fig. 10a and (Table 2), The As(V) adsorption onto OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was well fitted to Langmuir isotherm model, as demonstrated from the high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.98245). The maximum adsorption capacity (qmax 4.084 mg/g) suggests moderate adsorption performance. Langmuir constant b (88 L/g) reflects a moderate affinity between As(V) ions and OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs. This was further supported by the favorable separation factor (RL) values between 0.775 and 0.141, confirming the viability of the adsorption process under varying concentrations. These results demonstrate that the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs behaves as an effective monolayer nanosorbent for As(V) under the studied conditions [62]. The Freundlich isotherm describes adsorption on heterogeneous surfaces with varying site energies where the adsorption capacity increases with increasing concentration. Fig. 10b clarifies a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.98753) indicating a characterized adsorption process by multilayer adsorption onto a heterogeneous surface. The adsorption intensity mentioned in Table 2, is denoted by n = 1.85, suggesting a favorable adsorption mechanism, especially at lower arsenate concentrations. Additionally, the Freundlich constant (KFH = 478 L/g) signifies a moderate capacity for adsorption [63]. The comparison of R2 values indicates that As(V) adsorption onto OCQDs@Ca-Fe LDHs aligns more closely with the Freundlich isotherm than Langmuir isotherm model, implying multilayer adsorption onto heterogeneous surface.

Finally, the evaluated adsorption isotherms verified that the surface of the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs contributed to the As(V) removal mechanism through heterogeneous multilayers with varying site energies of accessible functional groups according to the Freundlich isotherm model. Moreover, Flory-Huggins isotherm was also verified an excellent model for this system to describe the surface heterogeneity of the nanosorbent to support the concept of non-uniform adsorption behavior and to reflect the strong affinity between the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs and As(V) ions, suggesting effective adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature and thermodynamic investigation
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of reaction temperature on the As(V) adsorptive removal by 150 mg OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was inspected through a temperature scale at 25–55 °C, using initial arsenate concentrations of 5 and 10 mg L−1 at pH 2.0 and shaking for 60 min. As depicted into (Fig. 11 ), the adsorption percentages of As(V) dropped from 87.73 % and 98.46 % to 81.32 % and 90.59 % respectively, in response to rising temperatures for 5 and 10 mg/L As(V), respectively. This signifies that higher temperatures adversely impact the adsorption capacity of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs due to several factors. Higher temperatures can shift the adsorption equilibrium, favoring desorption over adsorption, especially in exothermic processes [67]. Additionally, increased molecular motion at elevated temperatures enhances desorption rates and reduces contact time between As(V) ions and the nanosorbent, further lowering removal efficiency [68]. The structural integrity of the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs may also be compromised at higher temperatures, leading to fewer active sites for adsorption [32]. Moreover, increased solubility of As(V) ions at elevated temperatures may result in a higher concentrations of As(V) remaining in solution, while competing reactions to hinder arsenate adsorption [69].

As compiled in Table 3, the different conventional thermodynamic parameters namely, ΔG◦, ΔH◦ and ΔS◦ are calculated through Eqs. (2)–(4).
(2)

where R equals 8.314 J/mol⋅K and Kd​ (L/g) represents the constant of equilibrium sorption, calculated through:
(3)

In this situation, qe (mg/g) specifies the quantity of As(V) that has been adsorbed onto OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs at equilibrium, while Ce ​ (mg/L) stands for the equilibrium concentration of As(V). The thermodynamic parameters, (ΔH°) enthalpy and (ΔS°) entropy, are derived from the Van’t Hoff equation by plotting ln Kd against 1/T.
(4)

K d
= Δ S ° / R - Δ H ° / R T
The resulting slope and intercept from this plot yield the ΔH° and ΔS° values, as demonstrated in Fig. 12 . Thermodynamic inspection of As(V) adsorption onto OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs reveals key aspects of spontaneity and adsorption mechanism. At 5 mg/L concentration, positive values (ΔG°) ranging from 0.119 to 1.484 kJ/mol suggest a non-spontaneous process at this low concentration, aligning with weaker adsorbate-adsorbent interactions. However, at 10 mg/L, ΔG° values are negative (−8.476 to −3.104 kJ/mol), indicating spontaneous and more favorable adsorption reaction as concentration rises [70].

The computed enthalpy (ΔH°) values are −13.538 kJ/mol for 5 mg/L and −63.36 kJ/mol for 10 mg/L. These values, especially the larger negative value for the higher concentration, suggest that the adsorption process is based on chemisorptive removal of As(V) by OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs. Chemisorption generally involves stronger binding energies, with ΔH° ranging between −20 and −400 kJ/mol [71]. This also implies that the adsorption involves specific chemical interactions, likely driven by ion-exchange among As(V) ions and the inner carbonate anions in the adsorbent. Entropy (ΔS°) is negative at −45.83 J/mol K for 5 mg/L and −184.86 J/mol K for 10 mg/L, indicating a decreasing randomness of the solid − liquid interface during adsorption. Such decline in the disorder could be attached to the structured arrangement of As(V) ions onto the nanosorbent surface, supporting the chemisorption mechanism. The increased entropy at higher concentrations further supports the presence of varied energy sites, consistent with a heterogeneous surface adsorption process [72].

Generally, the findings of thermodynamic parameters strongly support that As(V) adsorption by OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was dominated by chemisorption process, driven by specific chemical interactions of both ion-pair and ion-exchange mechanism and particularly effective at lower temperatures and higher concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs in As(V) removal from real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs nanosorbent was assessed via its performance in As(V) removing from tap water and industrial discharge wastewater sourced from the Middle East Oil Refinery (MIDOR). 150 mg of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was mixed and agitated for 60 min with each sample (20 mL of 5 or 10 mg/L of As (V)) at pH 2.0 before being filtered. The results reveal that at initial concentration of 5 mg L−1, the adsorbent achieved an impressive removal efficiency of 97.8 % from tap water and 98.1 % from industrial wastewater. Additionally, at higher concentration (10 mg L−1), the removal values were further improved to 98.4 % and 98.6 %, respectively. These findings demonstrate that the investigated OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs nanosorbent effectively diminished the arsenic levels in the examined water matrices, highlighting its potential for practical application in treating arsenic-contaminated water, particularly in industrial settings where arsenic concentrations may be significant. Moreover, the evaluated OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs nanosorbent was compared versus other previously reported adsorbents as listed in Table 4 [73–79]. The high removal efficiencies indicate that this nanosorbent is a promising solution for addressing arsenic pollution in both domestic and industrial wastewater systems.

The reusability of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs nanosorbent after regeneration and elution by using 0.1 mol L−1 HCl demonstrated impressive stability and efficiency in the adsorption of As(V) over three regeneration cycles. The removal efficiencies were corresponded to 96.1 % after the first cycle, 94.8 % after the second cycle, and 90.3 % after the third cycle. These results indicate that the investigated OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs nanosorbent maintains its effectiveness and applicability for arsenic removal even after multiple regeneration cycles.

OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs nanosorbent was simply assembled by the direct binding and integrating olive leaf-derived quantum dots (OCQDs) with calcium (II) – iron (III) layered double hydroxides (Ca-Fe-LDHs) for the aim of implementation in removal of As(V) from aquatic systems. The assembled nanosorbent was verified in the nanoscale range (5.246–7.869 nm) with the presence of a number of identifiable functional groups, including metal oxyhydroxides, metal oxides and internal anions as well as mesoporous crystalline structure. OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was optimized under diverse controlling conditions of As(V) removal and the collected findings from this investigation referred to the following concluded points. (i) The pH 2 condition was labeled as the optimum removing condition of As(V) from aqueous solutions, achieving removal percentages as 77.8 % and 71.5 %, respectively at 5 and 10 mg/ L initial concentrations and 60 min. (ii) The removal percentages of As(V) were insignificantly influenced by the coexisting salts, demonstrating superior selectivity of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs attributed to both electrostatic attraction via ion-pair interaction and ion-exchange mechanisms between internal anions and As(V) ions. (iii) In presence of different ionic strengths using NaCl, the removal percentages of 5 mg/L As(V) solution was improved from 90.7 % to 96.1 %. A similar tendency was noted for 10 mg/L As (V) concentration, where the removal percentages increased from 87.6 % to 93.5 %. (iv) The temperature factor identified the interaction between As(V) and OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was exothermic and spontaneous, suggesting that the adsorption process is more favorable under lower temperature conditions and at higher concentrations. (v) Evaluation of the kinetic patterns showed that the best fitting to describe the interaction of As(V) with OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs was the pseudo-second order and Elovich models, indicating chemical interactions via both ion-pair and ion-exchange processes, while the intraparticle diffusion highlighted a complex mechanism involving both chemisorption and diffusion dynamics in the adsorption procedure. (vi) The evaluated adsorption isotherms verified that the surface of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs contributed to the As(V) removal mechanism via heterogeneous multilayers with accessible functional groups. (vii) OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs displayed excellent stability for regeneration by using HCl, giving 90.3 % after the third cycle and indicating its potential suitability for repeated arsenic remediation. (viii) OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs demonstrated effective removal of As(V) ions, achieving 97.8 % (5 mg L−1) and 98.4 % (10 mg L−1) from tap water, as well as 98.1 % (5 mg L−1) and 98.6 % (10 mg L−1) from wastewater to confirm the efficient uptake performance of OCQDs@Ca-Fe-LDHs.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Professor Mohamed E. Mahmoud has been ranked and listed in the Top 2% Scientists Worldwide (2020–2024). Professor Mahmoud is currently an Emeritus Professor in Analytical and Environmental Chemistry at Alexandria University, Alexandria, Egypt. He received his PhD in Analytical Chemistry from Northeastern University, MA, USA. Professor Mahmoud has published more than 265 publications in highly ranked journals and most of them are related to water treatment. His research interests are mainly focused on nanoscience and nanotechnology via assembly and synthesis of novel nanomaterials, nanosorbents and nanocomposites for potential application in multidiscipline fields as water treatment, radiation shielding, corrosion control and others.

Professor B.A. Abd-El-Nabey is currently an Emeritus Professor of Physical Chemistry at the Faculty of Science, Alexandria University, Alexandria, Egypt. Professor Abdel-Nabi has been appointed a full Professor since 1986 and his research interests are mainly focused on the fields of electrochemistry, polarography, corrosion inhibition, scale inhibition, coatings, material science and water treatment.

Dr. Marwa A. Abd-El-Fatah is currently working as an Assistant Professor of Physical Chemistry at Faculty of Education, Alexandria University, Alexandria, Egypt. She received her PhD degree from Faculty of science, Alexandria University at 2018. Her research interests include electrochemistry, adsorption studies, corrosion inhibition, scale inhibition, conversion coatings, material science and water treatment.

Mr. Amr Abdelrahman is a PhD researcher in Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Alexandria University, Alexandria, Egypt. His PhD research is focused on the fields of corrosion inhibition and water treatment by using novel nanomaterials and nanocomposites. Mr. Abdelrahman is also working as a Senior Chemist, Laboratory Department at “EPROM – MIDOR Refinery'' Alexandria, Egypt.
